# Homelab Network Setup — Working Log
# Follow top to bottom. Mark each step done with [x].

---

## PHASE 0 — SSH into the Pi from Your PC
# Do this once. After this, all Pi commands run from your Windows machine.

[ ] 1. Open PowerShell on the PC you're working from.

[ ] 2. Connect to the Pi:

        ssh loupi@192.168.50.1

[ ] 3. First connection will ask:

        Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no)?

    Type: yes → Enter

[ ] 4. Enter your Pi password when prompted.
    (Cursor won't move while typing — that's normal.)

[ ] 5. You should see a prompt like:

        pi@raspberrypi:~ $

    You're in. All Pi commands below run here.

---

## PHASE 1 — Fix DHCP Range on Pi
# Moves the DHCP pool to 100–200, leaves 10–99 clean for static devices.

[ ] 1. SSH into the Pi or open a terminal on it.

[ ] 2. Edit dnsmasq config:

        sudo nano /etc/dnsmasq.conf

[ ] 3. Find this line:

        dhcp-range=192.168.50.10,192.168.50.200,24h

    Change it to:

        dhcp-range=192.168.50.100,192.168.50.200,24h

    Save: Ctrl+O → Enter → Ctrl+X

[ ] 4. Restart dnsmasq:

        sudo systemctl restart dnsmasq

[ ] 5. Confirm it's running:

        sudo systemctl status dnsmasq

        (loupi@raspberrypi:~ $ sudo systemctl status dnsmasq
â— dnsmasq.service - dnsmasq - A lightweight DHCP and caching DNS server
     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/dnsmasq.service; enabled; preset: enabled)

             â””â”€override.conf
     Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-05-09 21:22:11 EDT; 21s ago
 Invocation: d8bfeb2132c04aeabcda90ba1743bdec
       Docs: man:dnsmasq(8)

    Process: 803465 ExecStart=/usr/share/dnsmasq/systemd-helper exec (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)

    Look for: Active: active (running)

---

## PHASE 2 — Static IP on PC-GAMING (Server)
# Target IP: 192.168.50.10

[ ] 6. On PC-GAMING:
    Settings → Network & Internet → Ethernet → click the connection → Edit (IP assignment)

[ ] 7. Switch to Manual. Turn on IPv4. Enter:

        IP address:   192.168.50.10
        Subnet mask:  255.255.255.0
        Gateway:      192.168.50.1
        DNS:          1.1.1.1
        Alt DNS:      8.8.8.8

[ ] 8. Click Save.

[ ] 9. Open PowerShell and confirm the IP took:

        ipconfig

    Look for: IPv4 Address . . . . . . . . . . . : 192.168.50.10

[ ] 10. Test internet:

        ping 1.1.1.1

---

## PHASE 3 — Static IP on SGamingPC (Workstation)
# Target IP: 192.168.50.11

[ ] 11. On SGamingPC — same steps as Phase 2, use these values:

        IP address:   192.168.50.11
        Subnet mask:  255.255.255.0
        Gateway:      192.168.50.1
        DNS:          1.1.1.1
        Alt DNS:      8.8.8.8

[ ] 12. Confirm:

        ipconfig

    Look for: 192.168.50.11

[ ] 13. Test internet:

        ping 1.1.1.1

---

## PHASE 4 — Connectivity Test
# Confirm both PCs can see each other.

[ ] 14. From PC-GAMING, ping the workstation:

        ping 192.168.50.11

[ ] 15. From SGamingPC, ping the server:

        ping 192.168.50.10

    Both should reply. If firewall blocks it, run this on the target PC:

        netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="Allow ICMP" protocol=icmpv4 dir=in action=allow

---

## PHASE 5 — Verify Pi Lease Table
# Confirm the old dynamic leases are gone and statics are in place.

[ ] 16. On the Pi:

        cat /var/lib/misc/dnsmasq.leases

    PC-GAMING and SGamingPC should no longer appear here
    (they have static IPs now, dnsmasq won't track them).

---

## STATUS

[x] Phase 0 complete — SSH working (ssh loupi@192.168.50.1)
[x] Phase 1 complete — DHCP range fixed (100–200)
[x] Phase 2 complete — PC-GAMING at 192.168.50.10
[x] Phase 3 complete — SGamingPC at 192.168.50.11
[x] Phase 4 complete — RDP working (local account: PC-GAMING\homelab)
[x] Phase 5 complete — Pi lease table clean

---
# Network foundation complete. Next: Hyper-V setup on PC-GAMING.
